coredata.dc:title: Adsorptions of lithium ion/atom and packing of Li ions on graphene quantum dots: Application for Li-ion battery
coredata.dc:creator: Pattarapongdilok, Naruwan
Parasuk, Vudhichai
coredata.dc:description: Adsorptions of lithium ion/atom and packing of Li ions on Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) were investigated using M06-2X/6-31g(d) level of theory. Three sizes of GQDs, C24H12, C54H18, and C96H24 were considered. To represent charging and discharging conditions, the GQD charge was set to −1 (charging), 0, and 1 (discharging). Both Li and Li+ prefer to be adsorbed on the C6 ring at the GQD rim. The adsorption energy depends on charge states and sizes of GQDs. For Li+, the adsorption on GQDs− gives the strongest interaction and is declined with the GQD size. For Li atom, it is the strongest on GQDs+ and increases with the size. When more Li ions are added, the adsorption energy is weakened owing to the ion-ion repulsion. In the charging condition, C24H12 and C54H18 can accommodate up to 4 and 5 ions, respectively, with the packing capacity of 0.055 and 0.025 ions/Å2.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene
Graphene quantum dots
Lithium ion
Lithium atom
Adsorption
DFT

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Two Li ions on the same side
Two Li ions on both sides

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: N. Pattarapongdilok would like to thank Center of Innovative Nanotechnology (CIN), Chulalongkorn University and Bansomdejchaopraya Rajabhat University for financial support. In addition, the support of computer resources and other facilities by the Center of Excellence in Computational Chemistry of Chulalongkorn University must be as well acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Adsorption sites of single Li/Li+ on three GQDs: (a) C24H12, (b) C54H18, (c) C96H24.
Adsorption patterns for on (a) four ions on C24H12 (q = −1); (b) two ions on C24H12 (q = 0); (c) five ions on C54H18 (q = −1); (d) three ions on C54H18 (q = 0).
Adsorption energy per ion (Eads/n) and number Li ions (n) for neutral and negatively charged C24H12 and C54H18.
Adsorption energy of Li atom on C24H12, C54H18, and C96H24 with q = −1, q = 0, and q = +1.
Net charges of Lithium atom and C6 ring of GQD, and total GQD.
Adsorption energy in kcal/mol of one Li ion on negative (q = −1), neutral (q = 0), and positive (q = +1) charge GQDs at various adsorbed sites.
Net charges of lithium ion, C6 ring of GQD and total GQD.

Basis-set superposition errors (BSSE) and BSSE-corrected adsorption energies (Eads + BSSE) both in kcal/mol for adsorption of Li ion on negatively charged (q = -1) and neutral (q = 0) coronene at possible adsorbed sites.
Adsorption energy per ion in kcal/mol and average net charge of lithium ions adsorbed on the same side of C24H12 and C54H18.
Adsorption energy per ion (Eads/n) in kcal/mol and average net charge of lithium ion on both sides of C24H12 and C54H18.
Adsorption energy per ion (Eads/n) in kcal/mol and average net charge of lithium ion on C24H12 and C54H18 .

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The highlight represents the structure with the lowest energy.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Ion-surface attraction (ELi + /GQD) and ion-ion repulsion (Erep) for neutral and negatively charged C24H12 and C54H18.

where a is the average bond length of carbon to carbon, b is the number of the hexagonal unit of GQD.

head.ce:title: Adsorptions of lithium ion/atom and packing of Li ions on graphene quantum dots: Application for Li-ion battery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanoscience and Technology Program, Graduate School, Chulalongkorn University, Bangkok 10330, Thailand
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nanoscience and Technology Program, Graduate School, Chulalongkorn University, Bangkok 10330, Thailand

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Science and Nanoscience and Technology Program

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Adsorptions of lithium ion/atom and packing of Li ions on Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) were investigated using M06-2X/6-31g(d) level of theory. Three sizes of GQDs, C24H12, C54H18, and C96H24 were considered. To represent charging and discharging conditions, the GQD charge was set to −1 (charging), 0, and 1 (discharging). Both Li and Li+ prefer to be adsorbed on the C6 ring at the GQD rim. The adsorption energy depends on charge states and sizes of GQDs. For Li+, the adsorption on GQDs− gives the strongest interaction and is declined with the GQD size. For Li atom, it is the strongest on GQDs+ and increases with the size. When more Li ions are added, the adsorption energy is weakened owing to the ion-ion repulsion. In the charging condition, C24H12 and C54H18 can accommodate up to 4 and 5 ions, respectively, with the packing capacity of 0.055 and 0.025 ions/Å2.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to high energy- and power-density recharging, flexible and lightweight design, and long lifetime, lithium-ion batteries have been applied for hybrid and plug-in hybrid electric vehicles and smart electronic storage [1–5]. The typical lithium-ion battery consists of a graphite anode and a transition-metal oxide cathode. Previous investigations indicated that small carbon sheets show higher performance than crystalline graphite [6,7]. The small size of nanomaterials plays a role in improving the performance of the lithium-ion battery. Because it provides a large surface area, short diffusion length, and fast diffusion rate. These conditions dramatically reduce Li+ diffusion time and allow quick absorption. Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs), a single- or few-layer of graphene having dimensions in the range of a few to ~100 nm [8,9], are perfect materials to be used as electrodes of lithium-ion batteries. These materials have been used as the composite member or coated materials in the lithium-ion battery [10,11]. Other potential properties of GQDs are high specific surface area, high mobility, good biocompatibility, strong luminescence, good hole transportability, high solubility in various solvents, low toxicity, etc. [10,12–16].

Adsorptions of lithium atom/ion on GQDs have been of interest by many researchers. However, they mainly focused on the adsorption of Li/Li+ onto hexagonal-shape GQDs, especially C24H12 or coronene [17–25]. It was reported that both Li and Li+ prefer to adsorb at the edge or the outermost circle of coronene [17]. Using DFT method, Harismesh et al. found that Li and Li+ prefer to adsorb on the neutral coronene at the center position and the hollow site of the hexagonal unit [24]. They also observed the adsorption energy of Li ion to be stronger than Li atom. Previous studies also concluded that there is no charge transfer between Li atom and coronene [20,22], while the charge transfer was observed for the case of negatively charged coronene and Li+ [22]. To understand the Li uptake of pyrolyzed organic solids at low temperature (T < 700 °C), Papanek et al. carried out semiempirical calculations for adsorption of Li atoms on coronene and circumcoronene [26]. They found six Li atoms binding on alternated peripheral rings of coronene and two Li atoms in the interstitial position forming Li8C24. For circumcoronene, twelve Li atoms were discovered at the outermost perimeter in an alternated manner while the other six Li ions are at the hollow sites on the second perimeter, yielding Li18C54. The high capacity of Li uptake is reasoned by the bonding between Li atom and a terminal carbon atom. This finding agrees with the study of Nakadaira et al. [27] who performed calculations on the adsorptions of Li ions over a small graphite cluster. They found that the adsorption of Li ion is depended on the termination of C atom at the outer perimeter. If C atoms are not saturated, Li ions prefer to form bond with C. Kurita et al. performed semiempirical calculations to study the nature of Li atom loading on circumcoronene and Si- and B-substituted circumcoronene [28]. They found the increase of the adsorption energy with the number of adsorbed Li atom and suggested that more Li atoms can be adsorbed on the Si-substituted circumcoronene than the B-substituted one. Fan et al. [29] studied Li adsorption on graphene sheet using periodic DFT calculations. The study reported that the cohesion energy per Li ion does not increase with the number of adsorbed Li ion due to complicated interactions. Also, the adsorption of Li ion on graphene is weak but a small Li cluster can be formed at high Li concentration.

Here we carried out Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations for adsorptions of Li/Li+ on three sizes of hexagonal-shape GQDs, i.e. C24H12 (coronene), C54H18 (circumcoronene), and C96H24 (circumcircum-coronene). Besides, for each GQD three charge states, q = −1, q = 0, and q = +1, were applied. The negative and positive charges of GQDs were modeled to represent the charging and discharging conditions of the battery, respectively. We are also interested in the capacity of GQDs in the loading of Li ion. Thus, adsorptions of multiple Li ions on GQDs were also studied.

All geometry optimizations were performed without any constraints using the Gaussian 09 package [30] at the M06-2X/6-31g(d) level of theory [31]. Rungnim et al. has validated M06-2X method with MP2 and CCSD(T) for the system of benzene dimers [32]. They found that the predicted binding energies differ from those of MP2 calculations by only 2–3%, while both M06-2X and MP2 methods significantly overestimate the binding energy in comparison to CCSD(T). This suggested that the M06-2X method can describe the dispersion interaction at the same level as the MP2 calculation. Therefore, the functional has been chosen in our study. Three sizes of GQDs, i.e. C24H12, C54H18, and C96H24, and each with the molecular charge of −1, 0, and +1 were studied. The negative and positive charges of GQDs, in respective order, represent charging and discharging conditions of Li-ion battery. Additionally, lithium ion or lithium atom was set to adsorb on the hollow position above the hexagonal unit of GQDs. This setup is in line with experimental evidence where LiC6 was found to be the stoichiometric number of Li+/graphite intercalation [33]. Adsorption positions for Li/Li+ on C24H12, C54H18, and C96H24 were displayed in Fig. 1 . To evaluate the basis-set convergence, basis-set superposition errors (BSSE) on the adsorption of Li+ on neutral and negatively charged C24H12 were estimated for two basis sets, i.e. 6-31g(d) and 6–311 g(d). The BSSE for such systems was performed using the counterpoise correction scheme [34].

The adsorption of the second Li ion or further can be either on the same side of the first ion representing by the number without prime or on the opposite side representing by number with prime. For example, (1,2) stands for the adsorption of the first Li ion at position 1 and the second ion at position 2 on the same side, while (1,2′) for the second ion at position 2 on the opposite side. Spin multiplicities of GQDs with q = 0 were set to singlet and GQDs with q =+1 and −1 were set to doublet. For Li and Li+, they are doublet and singlet, respectively. For GQD-Li complexes, the molecular charge comes from the combination of Li and GQD charges and the high spin state was set for all cases.

The adsorption energy (Eads) or binding affinity of Li/Li+ on GDQ was calculated using Eq. (1).
(1)
E ads
=
E
GQD - X
-
E GQD
+
E X
where EGQD-X = energy of GQD-Li/Li+ complex,
EGQD = energy of GQD,
EX = energy of dopant (Li/Li+).

Adsorption energies of Li atom on C24H12, C54H18, and C96H24 with negative (q = −1), neutral (q = 0), and positive (q = +1) charges were shown in Table 1 . Table 2 listed Mulliken’s net charge of Li, six-membered ring or C6 ring of GQD, and total GQD. There are two, four, and six possible adsorption positions for C24H12, C54H18, and C96H24, respectively. For all sizes and charges of GQDs, the adsorption of lithium atom at the outermost circle of GQDs or on the hexagonal ring at the edge of GQDs has the lowest adsorption energy and hence is the most favorite adsorption site. This observation is also supported by Umadevi and Sastry [17] who studied the adsorption of Li atom on coronene. On the contrary, the adsorption at the center is the least favorite. This behavior can be described by the electrostatic interaction. Since the C6 charge at the outermost circle is more negative than at the center position for all GQDs, see Table 2.

The adsorption of Li atom on positively charged GQDs showed the strongest interaction, while those on neutral and negatively charged GQDs are weaker. Adsorption energies of Li atom on positively charged GQDs are in the range of −21 to –32 kcal/mol, while those on neutral and negatively charged GQDs are between −8 and −29 kcal/mol and −3 and −26 kcal/mol, respectively. The size-dependent on the adsorption energy is also observed with the ordering (by magnitude) of C96H24 > C54H18 > C24H12 for all types of charged GQDs. This finding is in agreement with the report of Sadlej-Sosnowska [18].

From Table 2, the charge of Li atom is not zero but ranging from 0.3 to 0.53. For coronene, the charge of Li atom at the outermost circle of GQDs is more positive than at the center in agreement with the previous study by Umadevi and Sastry [17]. The positive charge means that Li atom donates electrons to GQD, and the number of transferred electrons is larger for GQDs with neutral and positive charge. However, this electron donation capability of Li atom is not size-dependent but rather charge (of GQD) dependent. This comment agrees with Umadevi and Sastry [17] and Tachikawa and Iyama [25] but disagrees with Ferre-Vilaplana [20] and Baker and Head-Gordon [22] who found no electron transfer for Li atom and neutral coronene.

Adsorption energies of Li+ on the three sizes of GQDs were given in Table 3 . The Mulliken’s net charges of Li ion and C6 ring of GQDs were listed in Table 4 . Similar to Li atom, Li ion also prefers to reside at the outermost circle of GQD’s ring and the adsorption at the center is the weakest, in accordance with previous investigations for coronene [17,23] and circumcoronene [23]. This is owing to the strong negative charge of C6 at the perimeter, see Table 4. The stronger negative charge of C6 at the edge is also shared by the report of Tachikawa [35]. When compared to Li atom, the binding of Li ion on GQDs is very much stronger matching with previous studies for C24H12 [24] and C96H24 [25]. From Table 3, among three charge states of GQDs, the binding of Li+ with negatively charged GQDs is the strongest with adsorption energy stronger than 100 kcal/mol. The adsorption energy is varied with the charge of GQD. The interaction between Li+ and GQDs becomes weaker as GQDs’ charges being more positive. For negatively charged GQDs the binding of Li+ is size-dependent and larger for the smaller size. Thus, the ordering of adsorption energies (by magnitude) is C24H12 > C54H18 > C96H24. The interaction between Li+ and neutral GQDs is in the range of −47 to −57 kcal/mol.

For neutral and negatively charged C24H12, we have estimated the basis-set superposition error (BSSE) for 6-31g(d) and 6-311g(d) basis sets and corrected binding energies for Li+ adsorptions. The results were listed in Table 5 . It can be observed from Table 5 that the BSSE depends on the basis set, adsorption position, and charge scheme of coronene. However, after BSSE correction and changing basis set we did not see any changes in the ordering of the adsorption energies. The position 2 remains the preferable adsorption site for Li ion on both neutral and negatively charged C24H12. The estimated BSSE is in the range of 1.53–2.17 kcal/mol for 6-31g(d) basis and 1.17–1.33 kcal/mol for 6-311g(d) basis. Although the basis-set convergence has not yet achieved at the 6-311g(d) level, the exact or real adsorption energy should be within 2.2 kcal/mol of the computed value. Therefore, we do not expect the lack of basis-set completeness to have any significant effects on our study.

For positively charged GQDs, Li+ can be adsorbed only on C96H24 with energies between −6 and −13 kcal/mol. For C24H12 + and C54H18 +, positive adsorption energies were obtained, suggesting the unfavorable adsorption. We observed the opposite size-dependent ordering for adsorption energies of neutral and positively charged GQDs, which is C96H24 > C54H18 > C24H12. The larger size has stronger adsorption. The adsorption between Li+ and GQDs could be reasoned from the electrostatic interaction between Li+ and GQDs. However, the binding between Li ion and the surface depends on the local charge, not the molecular charge. Since the total charges of GQDs and GQDs+ are positive, but negative binding energies were observed for most cases.

Noting that the charge of Li+ is approximately 0.5 not 1 for all cases. Thus, there is the electron transfer from the GQD to lithium ion. This finding is in agreement with the result of Baker and Gordon [22], Umadevi and Sastry [17], and Tachikawa and Iyama [25]. However, they only considered the case of negatively charged coronene in their work. The electron donating capability of GQD depends more on its charge than size. The electron donating capability of GQDs decreases, when the GQD charge becomes more positive. Comparing to the case of Li atom, in which GQD acts as an electron acceptor, for this case GQDs behave as electron donors. Thus, GQDs can become both electron donors and acceptors depending on the adsorbate.

In the discharging condition, Li ion is strongly attached to the electrode. When the polarity of the electrode is changed to positive, Li+ could be dispelled and/or replaced by Li atom. This is reverse in the charging condition. The interplay between Li and Li+ during the operation of the battery can be realized.

One of the key properties of Li-ion battery is the high charge density. Thus, Li can be packed densely on the electrode. Since Li ions have charge and they repulsed each other, we are, therefore, interested in the adsorption of multiple lithium ions on GQDs. After the first adsorption, the second Li ion can be resided either on the same side of the plane as the first ion or on the opposite side. Here, the adsorption of two Li ions on GQDs was monitored and thoroughly discussed. Later, more Li ions were added to determine the maximum number of Li ions adsorbed on GQDs and preferable adsorption patterns. Two models of GQDs, C24H1, and C54H18, with three charge states (q = -1, 0, and 1) were used for the study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Two Li ions on the same side
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption of two Li ions on the same side gives 4 and 19 possible adsorption patterns for C24H12, and C54H18, respectively. The adsorption energy per ion (Eads/n) and the average net charge of Li ion on C24H12 and C52H18 in various charge schemes for all possible patterns were listed in Table 6 . For some adsorption patterns, we cannot get calculations to converge. This is probably because the system is highly repulsed. (Notice that there are systems where Li ions are placed close to each other.) We also found that for many adsorption patterns Li+ has been migrated from the starting position to near edge of GQDs after the geometry optimization.

For all sizes of GQDs, Eads/n for this case is smaller than that of the one-ion adsorption. Comparing GQDs of the same sizes and adsorption patterns, Eads/n is in the following order, GQD+ > GQD > GQD−. (The larger value means less negative or the binding is weaker.) For all charge states and sizes of GQDs, the adsorption pattern where two Li ions are most far apart provides the strongest Eads/n. Thus, for the adsorption of two Li ions on the same size, the ions prefer to be at least one hexagonal unit away. This conclusion agrees with the work of Papanek et al. who study adsorptions of Li atom on coronene and circumcoronene [26]. As mentioned for the case of one Li ion, the electrostatic interaction plays an important part for the adsorption energy. In this case, apart from the interaction between Li+ and GQDs, there is also ion-ion repulsion. The effect of the repulsion is more emphasized for the positively charged GQDs, where all adsorption patterns give positive adsorption energies. For negatively charged GQDs, it might seem that the interaction between Li+ and GQDs could overcome the ion-ion repulsion. However, when compared with the case of one Li ion the overall interaction is still much weaker. We also observed the reduction of the Li-ion net charges as the charge on the GQD decreases, i.e. qLi(GQD-) < qLi(GQD) < qLi(GQD+). The electron-rich GQD (GQD−) can better donate electrons to Li ions, which is resulted in the diminishing of the repulsion between ions. As a result, there is a stronger adsorption for Li ion on GQD−. Our result suggests that GQDs could better attract Li+ when the electrode has a high negative charge.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Two Li ions on both sides
ce:section.ce:para: From our study on cases of one Li ion and two Li ions on the same side, we knew that the Li ion prefers to bind to the hexagonal unit at the perimeter of the GQD and two Li ions should be placed at least one hexagonal unit away from each other. Adopting this information, there are 5 and 12 possible adsorption patterns, respectively, for the case of two-ion adsorption on both sides of C24H12 andC54H18. Table 7 showed Eads/n and the average net charge of Li ion adsorbed on C24H12 and C54H18 in various charge schemes and adsorption patterns.

From Table 7, the adsorption of two Li ions on both sides gives a similar trend to that on the same side. The binding of Li ion and GQD− is the strongest following by that on GQD, while there is no binding observed for that on GQD+. The adsorption interaction for this case is slightly stronger than the adsorption of two Li ions on the same side. However, the most preferable adsorption patterns are similar. Thus, two Li ions try to be the furthest away from each other. This suggested that the adsorption of Li+, in this case, is also governed by the ion-ion repulsion, which is supported by the reduction of the net charge of Li ion and the increase of Eads/n. Interestingly, we can put two Li ions on the same hexagonal unit but the opposite side without strong repulsion. This is probably because the electron density of GQDs helps to shield the positive charge of Li ions and explains the stronger binding in the case of two Li ions on both sides.
To investigate the loading capacity of Li ions on GQDs, we added more Li ions to both sides of the GQD and determined Eads/n as shown in Table 8 .

Adsorption patterns as given in Table 8 were acquired by the following. First, we used structures obtained from Section 3.2. Then, the third ion was added to the hexagonal unit on the surface at all possible positions while keeping the non-adjacent rule. Geometry optimizations were performed for the prepared structures. For some models, ions cannot be held on the surface and we cannot obtain converged results. Those structures were discarded. We, then, repeated the procedure for other multiple-ion adsorptions.

Also, the average net charges of Li ion were given in Table 8. We cannot get converged solutions for systems that included more than 5 and 6 ions to negatively charged C24H12 and C54H18, respectively. For neutral GQDs, we can get the converged solution only for systems that contain 3 ions on C24H12 and 4 ions on C54H18. However, only two adsorption patterns of C54H18 have negative Eads/n, (3,13,17) and (3′,13,17). Interestingly, these two patterns have Li ions at the same positions and Li ions are most far apart. We did not perform calculations for systems with more than two Li ions on positively charged GQDs. Because we obtained positive binding energies for the adsorption of one and two Li ions on GQDs+. Examples of the adsorption of multiple Li ions on GQDs were depicted in Fig. 2 . The figure shows that most of Li ions favor adsorbing at the edge region of GQD and two Li ions prefer to be at the furthest position from each other, in agreement with results of Nakadaira et al. [27] and Fan et al. [29]. Moreover, no more than three ions can be placed on the same side of GQD. The average Li-Li distance on the GQD is no less than 6.6 Å. This suggests that the packing of Li+ is controlled by the ion-ion repulsion.
To determine the maximum loading capacity of GQDs, Eads/n for different adsorption patterns on C24H12 and C54H18 were plotted against numbers of Li ions, Fig. 3 .

From Fig. 3, neutral and negatively charged C24H12 can adsorb up to 2 and 4 Li ions, respectively, and still maintain negative adsorption energy. Whereas for C54H18, using positive adsorption energy criteria neutral and negatively charged GQDs can load up to 3 and 5 Li ions, respectively. These numbers are the maximum loading capacity for Li ions of GQDs. Therefore, negatively charged GQDs can pack more ions than neutral ones and larger GQDs can adsorb more Li ions than smaller ones. The Li adsorption can be all on one side and both sides. However, the latter is more preferable. Intriguingly, we noticed that plots between Eads/n and numbers of Li ions (n) for all systems are linear with R2 greater than 0.9 for all systems. From the plot, values of Eads/n seem to be dependent on the charge not the size of GQD. However, the slope of the graph for each system varies with the size of GQD and is invariant with its charge.

The importance of electrostatic interactions between Li ion and the charge on C6 ring for the single ion adsorption (attraction) and the repulsion between Li ions for the two-ion adsorption hints us to rewrite the linear relation between Eads/n and n in terms of attraction and repulsion interactions as
(2)
E
a d s
/ n =
E
L i
+
/ G Q D
+
n - 1
2
E
rep
where
E
L i
+
/ G Q D
: ion-surface attraction energy (kcal/mol), and
E
r e p
: ion-ion repulsion energy (kcal/mol).
From Fig. 3, values of ELi + /GQD and Erep for neutral and negatively charged C24H12 and C54H18 were determined and reported in Table 9 .

ELi+/GQD and Erep are dependent on both size and charge of GQD, but more on the charge. Values of ion-surface attraction agree with adsorption energies of the single-ion adsorption. The behavior of ELi+/GQD with size and charge could be explained similarly to the case of one-Li-ion adsorption. Thus, the negatively charged GQD has a stronger ion-surface attraction. The ion-ion repulsion is varied with the size and to a lesser extent with the charge of GQD. The larger GQD has a smaller Erep owing to the larger distance between ions. An interesting note is that Erep is almost one-half of ELi+/GQD for negatively charged GQDs, but it is equal to ELi+/GQD for neutral ones. Thus, more Li ions can be packed on negatively charged GQDs.
To compare the loading capacity of C24H12 and C54H18 for Li ions, the numbers of Li ions per surface area of GQDs were computed and given in Table 10 .

The loading capacity reflects how dense that Li ions can be packed on the GQD. The smaller GQD has a higher packing capacity than the larger one for all charge states. Li ions can be packed more densely on small GQDs during the charging condition (q = −1). Therefore, small GQDs would be more suitable for use as an electrode in the Li-ion battery.

Li/Li+ prefers to be adsorbed at the outermost circle of GQDs for all sizes and charges. The adsorption energy of Li/Li+ on GQD is varied with its amount and the size and charge of GQD. For Li+, the adsorption on negatively charged GQDs gives the strongest adsorption energy, −135 kcal/mol for C24H12. This is followed by neutral and positively charged GQDs. For positively charged GQDs, positive adsorption energies were found, while negative adsorption energies were observed only for C96H24 +. Thus, Li+ cannot bind to small positively charged GQDs. For Li atom, the interaction with positively charged GQDs is the strongest (–32 kcal/mol for C96H24 +), following by neutral and negatively charged GQDs. However, the interaction is much weaker than those for Li ion. This supports the adsorptions of Li+/Li on the electrode during the charging/discharging condition. The size-dependent of the adsorption energy on negatively charged GQD by Li+ is C24H12 > C54H18 > C96H24. While for other systems, the ordering is C96H24 > C54H18 > C24H12. The charge transfers between GQD and Li/Li+ is observed. GQDs can be both electron donor and acceptor depending on the adsorbate (donor for Li+ and acceptor for Li). For the multiple-Li-ion adsorption, the ion-ion repulsion controls the adsorption in addition to Li+-GQD interaction. The adsorption of the second Li ion can be either on the same side or opposite side of the GQD sheet. Two Li ions can be positioned on the same hexagonal unit but the opposite side of the plane or at least one hexagonal unit away on the same side. The adsorption becomes even weaker as more Li ions being added. For neutral and positively charged GQDs, the addition of Li ions beyond 3 ions becomes unfavorable. Unlike negatively charged GQDs, the material can comfort up to 5 ions. The adsorption of Li ion on GQDs can be described by ion-surface attraction and ion-ion repulsion. For negatively charged GQDs, ion-surface attractions are −130 and −115 kcal/mol for C24H12 and C54H18, respectively. While ion-ion repulsion also in the respective order is 69 and 53 kcal/mol. For neural GQD, they are −48/−50 kcal/mol and 65/50 kcal/mol for ion-surface attraction and ion-ion repulsion on C24H12/C54H18, respectively. The small GQD has a higher loading capacity than the larger one. The loading capacity also depends on the charge of GQDs. Negatively charged GQDs can pack Li ions more densely. Small GQD is suggested to be used as electrode material for Li-ion batteries.

Our observation on the preferred adsorption sites of Li/Li+ and dependent of the adsorption energy on size and charge of GQD is shared by previous theoretical works of Umadevi and Sastry [17] and Tachikawa [35]. The existence of the charge transfer between Li/Li+ and GQD is also agreed by Umadevi and Sastry [17] and Tachikawa and Iyama [25]. While Ferre-Vialplana [20] and Baker and Head-Gordon [22] did not find any charge transfer between Li and GQD. For multiple-ion adsorption, we found that Li+ prefers to be placed on the alternated hexagon unit in agreement with Papanek et al. [26]. Thus, our work is in line with previous theoretical works.
Naruwan Pattarapongdilok: Investigation, Writing - original draft, Data curation. Vudhichai Parasuk: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing, Resources, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: N. Pattarapongdilok would like to thank Center of Innovative Nanotechnology (CIN), Chulalongkorn University and Bansomdejchaopraya Rajabhat University for financial support. In addition, the support of computer resources and other facilities by the Center of Excellence in Computational Chemistry of Chulalongkorn University must be as well acknowledged.
